title: Coconut milk cold-process soap
subtitle: A gentle, creamy bar using full-fat coconut milk as the lye liquid
excerpt: Full-fat coconut milk replaces the distilled water in this formula, adding conditioning fats and sugars that produce a rich, creamy lather. The finished bar is ivory to pale cream and pairs well with a light tropical or citrus scent.
sourceNotes: Coconut milk soap technique from Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997) and Soap Queen (soapqueen.com, May 2026). SAP values from SoapCalc. Coconut milk fat content from USDA nutritional database.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 kg soap batter (~7 x 130 g bars after 4-week cure)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally and recalculate lye. Freeze the coconut milk before use regardless of batch size.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Freeze the coconut milk in the lye pitcher the day before. This is not optional; adding lye to liquid coconut milk causes scorching.
recipe.temperatureNote: The frozen coconut milk will absorb the heat of the lye reaction. Allow the resulting mixture to warm to 38 to 42 degrees Celsius before combining with oils.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 18 months from cure date in a cool, dry place. Coconut milk soaps are prone to dreaded orange spots (DOS) because of the residual milk fats and sugars. Store on a rack with good air circulation. Discard if the bar smells rancid or shows orange spotting.

recipeTools.notes: Freeze the coconut milk in this pitcher the day before

glossaryTerms.definition: The reaction between sodium hydroxide and fats or oils that produces soap and glycerol. Milk sugars and fats participate in the reaction and contribute to the colour and conditioning properties of the finished bar.
The percentage of oils left unsaponified. At 5% this formula leaves conditioning free fatty acids in the bar.

The point where blended oils and lye solution have emulsified to the consistency where a drizzle leaves a visible trail on the surface. Coconut milk batters can reach trace faster than water batches because of the dissolved sugars.
Orange or brown spots on a cured soap bar caused by rancid oils or reaction between residual sugars and atmospheric oxygen. Common in milk soaps stored in warm or humid conditions.

content.text: Full-fat tinned coconut milk contains 17 to 24% fat. Those fats carry into the soap and contribute to a rich, conditioning lather. Temperature management is the critical step with any milk-based lye solution. Adding sodium hydroxide to liquid coconut milk at room temperature scorches the sugars instantly, darkens the batter and produces an unpleasant smell. Freeze the coconut milk solid before adding lye. This absorbs the heat, keeps the temperature manageable, and protects the milk.
of the coconut milk fats adds to the conditioning properties of the bar alongside the olive and castor oil base.
This recipe uses sodium hydroxide. Follow the
guidance before starting. Wear protective goggles and gloves throughout. The coconut milk must be completely frozen before you add lye; do not skip this step.

items.prepNote: 35% of oil weight; melt if solid
Run through a lye calculator to confirm
Full-fat tinned; weighed, poured into the HDPE pitcher, and frozen solid the day before

content.text: The day before: weigh the coconut milk into the HDPE pitcher and freeze until completely solid. Follow the full

With the frozen coconut milk in the pitcher, add the sodium hydroxide in small increments, stirring each addition until dissolved before adding the next. The frozen milk absorbs heat. The mixture will heat and the coconut milk will melt as the lye dissolves. Keep the temperature below 50 degrees Celsius by slowing down the addition if needed. Allow the lye mixture to cool to 38 to 42 degrees Celsius.
Melt and cool the oils to the same temperature range. Pour the lye mixture into the oils and blend to
. Pour into the mould and leave undisturbed for 48 hours. Unmould, cut into bars, and cure on a rack for a minimum of 4 weeks.

because of the residual milk fats. Store on a rack with good air circulation away from direct light. Discard any bar that smells rancid or shows orange spotting.
